one_line_takeaway: A training-free scheme that uses an LLM paraphraser plus vocab-permutation and orthogonal logit perturbation to embed watermarks that stay verifiable through paraphrasing, re-watermarking, in-context use, and even downstream fine-tuning, enabling scalable multi-client text IP protection and LLM data provenance.
key_finding: The permutation+orthogonal-perturbation design gives watermarks that are simultaneously far more scalable, more robust to LLM-based attacks, and much cheaper to verify than prior linguistic watermarking. On articles at kappa=6 WATERFALL reaches AUROC 0.992 at STS 0.887, and crucially retains AUROC 0.951/0.881/0.815/0.775 under translation/paraphrase/re-watermark/in-context attacks, where baselines M-BIT and P-NLW collapse toward chance (as low as 0.363 and 0.475). Its combinatorial ID capacity (~10^130274) dwarfs the ~10^3 and ~10^10 effective capacities of M-BIT/P-NLW. It transfers zero-shot to code (beating SRCMARKER on verifiability) and, most notably for the security frame, watermarks survive downstream fine-tuning: a client can verify unauthorized training with AUROC 1.0 using ~100 queries even when 100 clients' data are mixed into the training set.
core_contribution: WATERFALL is presented as the first training-free framework for robust and scalable *data-centric* text watermarking and LLM data provenance. Its own novelty (distinct from cited work) is: (1) being the first to use an LLM as a paraphraser to embed watermarks into pre-existing, human-owned text (rather than watermarking an LLM's own freshly-generated output, which the paper explicitly frames as the separate model-centric line of work of Kirchenbauer et al. 2023 etc.); and (2) a watermarking operator W that combines vocab permutation P (a key-seeded pseudorandom permutation of the token space Vo into a watermarking space Vw) with an orthogonal perturbation F that adds an orthogonal-basis-function signal (e.g., Fourier) to the logits in Vw before mapping back with P^-1. Applying the same signal across many n-gram carrier units, while the LLM paraphraser corrects semantic deviations in later steps, yields watermarks that survive paraphrasing, re-watermarking, in-context use, and even fine-tuning of a downstream LLM. The authors also formulate the robust-scalable watermarking problem with a desiderata set (fidelity, verifiability, robust verifiability, scalability) and an attack taxonomy A1-A5. Note: 'BASIC' (synonym-substitution) is an illustrative strawman the authors define for exposition, not a contribution; M-BIT, P-NLW and SRCMARKER are external baselines, not this paper's methods.
threat_model: Adversaries possess watermarked text T_w (with a client's secret ID mu they do not know) and want to strip/overwrite the watermark or claim the IP while preserving the text's semantic content c. Verification must not require access to the original text To and must not extract mu as a subroutine (so it cannot be reused by attackers). Five attack classes: A1 word insertion/deletion/synonym substitution; A2 translation and LLM paraphrasing; A3 re-watermarking with a different watermark to overwrite the original; A4 using T_w for in-context prompting of any LLM; A5 using T_w to fine-tune a third-party black-box LLM (unauthorized training). Attacks also include converting text to audio/handwriting to defeat format-based (homoglyph/Unicode/metadata) watermarks. The security goal is IP enforcement / provenance: proving ownership and detecting unauthorized use of copyrighted text including in LLM training.
limitations: Author-stated: not applicable where IP lies in style/format (e.g., poems, lyrics, a national anthem's exact wording) since paraphrasing alters those, nor to very short text (a few tokens). Reviewer-observed within the paper's own text: there is an inconsistency between the body (states llama-2-13b as the article paraphraser) and footnote 4 (links Llama-2-7b-chat-hf), so the exact article-paraphraser model is ambiguous. Robust-verifiability numbers, while far above baselines, still degrade meaningfully under the strongest attacks (A4 in-context drops to 0.775), so verification is probabilistic rather than guaranteed. Article evaluation uses a single mid-size open LLM paraphraser and the data-provenance experiment fine-tunes only a small model (gpt2-xl via LoRA), leaving robustness against large modern or adversarially-aware models untested in the main text. Many key details (large-scale deployment, additional attacks, targeted-overlap A3) are deferred to appendices.

datasets_used: c4 realnewslike (news articles, mean length 412 tokens / ~355 words) for article data-ownership
MBJSP (multi-lingual JavaScript code benchmark) for code watermarking, fidelity via pass@10
ArXiv abstracts dataset (topic-categorized, 4000 texts per client ID) for LLM data provenance
quantitative_results: Articles kappa=6: mean AUROC 0.992 at STS 0.887; AUROC 0.98 even at 100-token texts
Baselines (article): M-BIT STS 0.998 / AUROC 0.987; P-NLW STS 0.942 / AUROC 0.882
Robust verifiability AUROC (WATERFALL) Pre 0.992, A2-translate 0.951, A2-paraphrase 0.881, A3-overwrite 0.815, A4-in-context 0.775
Robust verifiability AUROC (P-NLW) 0.885/0.475/0.508/0.724/0.502; (M-BIT) 0.988/0.567/0.363/0.664/0.525 across same attacks
Scalability: max |M| = |F1|*|V|! ~ 10^130274 (|V|=32000, Fourier basis); M-BIT ~9.5 bits (~10^3), P-NLW ~23.2 bits (~10^10)
1st-percentile AUROC over 1000 competing IDs: WATERFALL 0.976, M-BIT 0.614, P-NLW 0.766
Metadata extraction (kp in 1..31999): accuracy 48% -> 99% when combining 5 pieces of text at kappa=6 (random guess 0.003125%)
Compute (Table 2, RTX A5000): watermark 24.8s (M-BIT 2.97s, P-NLW 147s); verification 0.035s on 16-core CPU, 75x faster than M-BIT (2.61s) and 4237x faster than P-NLW (148s)
Code (Table 3, kappa=3): WATERFALL pass@10 0.969, AUROC pre-attack 0.904 / post-A2 0.718, scalability 10^130274 vs SRCMARKER pass@10 0.984, AUROC 0.726 / 0.662, scalability 10^5
LLM data provenance: AUROC 1.0 with ~100 queries to the fine-tuned model, holding even at M=100 mixed clients (M=20 and M=100 curves overlap)
baselines_compared: M-BIT (Yoo et al. 2023) - deep-learning linguistic watermarking, articles
P-NLW (Qiang et al. 2023) - deep-learning linguistic watermarking, articles
SRCMARKER (Yang et al. 2024, 16-bit config) - SOTA code watermarking
BASIC - authors' own illustrative synonym-substitution strawman (conceptual, not a run baseline)
